one_line_takeaway: A 21-model safety benchmark showing VLLMs are robust to OOD images but brittle to OOD/counterfactual text and to a simple CLIP-ViT misleading attack, and that visual instruction tuning measurably weakens the safety alignment inherited from the base LLM.
key_finding: VLLMs handle OOD *visual* content well (>95% Yes/No accuracy on OODCV images for most models) but are fragile to OOD *text*: counterfactual suffixes drop overall accuracy by an average 17.1% and Yes/No accuracy by 33.2%. On adversarial robustness, the authors' simple single-ViT MIX.ATTACK beats the prior ensemble-based ATTACKBARD by an average 8.4% missing rate (SIN.ATTACK by 5.0%) at epsilon=64/255. Crucially, vision-language training erodes LLM safety: VLLMs show 17.3% higher white-box jailbreak ASR than their base LLMs, and adversarial strings transfer from LLM->VLLM with 26.0% higher ASR than VLLM->LLM. Vision-only jailbreak, however, transfers poorly (only ~5% increase across models), so it is not yet a universal attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a comprehensive safety-evaluation benchmark for Vision Large Language Models (VLLMs) spanning two axes: (1) out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization via two new VQA datasets each with a variant -- OODCV-VQA (+OODCV-Counterfactual) built on OODCV images and Sketchy-VQA (+Sketchy-Challenging) built on sketch images; and (2) adversarial robustness / redteaming, comprising the authors' own simple CLIP-ViT-based misleading attack (SIN.ATTACK and MIX.ATTACK) plus benchmarking of two prior jailbreak strategies targeting the vision encoder vs. the language component. They evaluate 21 models (20 open-source VLLMs across 10 families + GPT-4V) and report five findings. This is the paper's OWN contribution; the vision-side jailbreak (Qi et al. style) and the LLM-side GCG attack (Zou et al.) are prior methods the paper benchmarks, not invents.
threat_model: Two adversary settings. (1) Misleading attack: a white-box adversary perturbs an input image (L-infinity bounded, epsilon0 = 32/255 or 64/255) to maximize CLIP image-text similarity to an irrelevant target phrase, causing the VLLM to emit visual-unrelated descriptions that drop the true object label. (2) Jailbreak: an adversary induces toxic/harmful outputs either by (a) optimizing an adversarial image on the vision encoder to elicit toxic content (white-box and transfer), or (b) appending GCG-style adversarial suffixes to toxic text instructions fed to the LLM part, tested in vanilla / white-box / transfer (LLM<->VLLM, and transfer to GPT-4V) configurations. OOD scenarios are a robustness (not attacker) setting.
limitations: GPT-4V results are on small, adversarially-curated subsets (100-650 examples) with human-in-the-loop judging, so its numbers are not directly comparable to open models' full-set scores. Toxicity/harmfulness rely on automated judges (Perspective API threshold 0.5; GPT-3.5 classifier) which are noisy proxies. OOD questions are template-generated, potentially limiting linguistic diversity. The misleading attack is white-box on CLIP-ViT-L-14 and its transfer is uneven (e.g., CogVLM is 34.3% below average missing rate, attributed to its 17B vision stack; Fuyu lacks an explicit ViT). Models evaluated are the 2023 generation (LLaVA, MiniGPT4, InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl, Qwen-VL, CogVLM, InternLM-X, Fuyu, GPT-4V).

datasets_used: OODCV-VQA (new; built on OODCV images; 1,400 Yes/No + 2,844 digit = 4,244 instances across 7 OOD conditions)
Sketchy-VQA (new; 50 most-frequent classes, 40 images each; 2,000 images / 4,000 Yes/No VQA instances)
Misleading-attack set of 200 images sourced from NIPS17 adversarial-attack dataset, annotated by 2 annotators
GCG toxic-behavior set (100 sampled behaviors) for LLM-part jailbreak
quantitative_results: OODCV-VQA overall accuracy: GPT-4V 80.61 (best), Qwen-VL-Chat 76.07, CogVLM 76.00, InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B 74.92; most models >95% on Yes/No
OODCV-Counterfactual causes average 17.1% overall accuracy drop and 33.2% Yes/No drop; GPT-4V drops 11.6% overall and 14.1% on counting; PandaGPT and Fuyu collapse to ~2% Yes/No
Sketchy-VQA F1: best open model InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B ~83.2%, GPT-4V ~69.2% (challenging subset); less-common objects cause avg 4.4% F1 drop; best F1 stays below 70%

Misleading attack (missing rate, epsilon=64/255): MIX.ATTACK beats ATTACKBARD by avg 8.4%, SIN.ATTACK by 5.0%; MIX.ATTACK reaches 87.5% on LLaVA-Vicuna-v0-7B; CogVLM only 35.0% (34.3% below average); GPT-4V mostly rejects (30% missing / 48% rejection under MIX.ATTACK)
Vision-encoder jailbreak: direct attack yields 2.1x more toxic outputs than clean images, but only ~5% average increase when transferred across all VLLMs (limited transferability)

LLM-part jailbreak ASR (Table 7): white-box avg LLM 77.5% vs VLLM 94.8% (+17.3%); vanilla avg LLM 1.3% vs VLLM 6.8% (+5.5%); LLM->VLLM transfer 26.0% higher ASR than VLLM->LLM; transfer to GPT-4V low (~3-5% ASR)
baselines_compared: ATTACKBARD (Dong et al. 2023 ensemble-based Bard image attack) -- baseline for the misleading attack
Clean images and Gaussian/random-noise images -- lower-bound baselines for missing rate
Prior vision-encoder jailbreak (Qi et al. style visual adversarial examples) -- benchmarked, not proposed
GCG automatic LLM attack (Zou et al.) -- benchmarked for the LLM-part jailbreak
Base LLMs vs. their VLLM counterparts (Vicuna-v0/v1.5-7B/13B, LLaMA-Chat-13B) -- to isolate the safety effect of vision-language tuning
